title: Colour over pyrography
subtitle: Applying watercolour washes over burned wood for a mixed-media effect
excerpt: Colour washes applied over pyrography burns combine the precision of burned line work with the warmth of pigment. The burned lines resist the watercolour wash, creating a natural resist effect. This technique covers wash preparation, application sequence, and how to maintain the tonal structure of the pyrography beneath the colour.
sourceNotes: Traditional British arts-and-crafts pyrography colouring practice; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for tinted pokerwork methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: The art of decorating wood by burning the surface with a heated metal tip; the burned marks form the line structure over which colour is applied.
A diluted watercolour or ink applied to wood with a brush; the wash pools in the wood grain and settles around the burned marks without obscuring them.
The phenomenon where burned wood repels watercolour slightly, keeping the darkest burned marks visible through the colour layer.

content.text: Work in a well-ventilated room; burning wood produces light smoke. Colour over
pyrography

is not the same as painting on wood: the burned marks are already the drawing, and the colour is a transparent wash that adds warmth and depth without obscuring the structure. The technique works because burned carbon on wood repels water-based colour slightly; a natural
resist effect
that keeps the darkest burned marks visible and separate from the wash above them.
The pyrography base

The pyrography must be complete and fully cooled before any colour is applied. The base should be a complete composition with tonal structure; light, mid, and dark zones established. Pale sycamore or basswood works best for colour pyrography: the pale base allows the colour to read clearly. The burned line work should be slightly darker overall than you intend the final piece, because the colour wash will add a tint to the pale background areas and slightly reduce the apparent contrast.
The colour

Watercolour pencils dissolved in a small amount of water produce a controllable wash. Dilute watercolour paint (tube or pan) also works. The colour should be diluted to a very pale wash; 90 percent water, 10 percent pigment for the first coat. Wood absorbs water differently across the grain direction, which produces subtle variation in the wash depth: this is part of the character of the technique, not a fault to correct.
Application sequence

Dampen the panel surface first with a brush of clean water. This opens the grain slightly and helps the wash spread evenly rather than puddling at the wood surface. Allow the panel to absorb the water for 30 seconds before applying the colour wash.
Apply the first colour wash with a wide soft brush in a single pass across the surface. Do not brush back and forth; work in one direction. The
wash
should be transparent enough to read the pyrography through it immediately. Leave to dry completely; 20 to 30 minutes.

Assess the colour depth after the first wash dries. The colour will dry 30 to 50 percent lighter than it looks when wet. If the colour needs deepening, apply a second wash in the same colour or a slightly richer mix.

Apply accent colours to specific areas if needed: a second colour for a leaf, a different hue for the background. Each colour zone should be painted wet-on-dry (the previous wash fully dry before the next is applied) to maintain clean colour boundaries.

Allow the final wash to dry completely, then assess whether the pyrography structure is still visible. If the darkest burned marks have lost contrast under the colour, a final pass with the writing tip at high temperature can re-establish them without disturbing the dried colour.
Sealing the finished piece

Once the final wash is dry, seal the surface with a thin coat of shellac or matte varnish applied with a soft brush. Do not use Danish oil over colour pyrography; the oil solvent can lift water-based colour from the wood surface. Shellac sets in 2 to 3 hours and provides a durable, non-yellowing seal over the combined pyrography and colour layer.
Common mistakes
Colour too saturated on the first coat: a strong first wash soaks into the grain and cannot be removed without sanding the surface. Always start with a very pale first wash and build depth gradually.

Applying oil sealer before the colour dries: oil repels the water-based colour and produces a blotchy surface. Allow all colour washes to dry fully before sealing, and use shellac rather than oil as the final seal.
Brushing back and forth over a drying wash: re-wetting a partially dry wash pulls the colour and produces streaks. Apply in one direction per pass and leave to dry before applying the next wash.
